steps.text: Extract representative gas samples from an emission vent or stack.
Condition the gas if necessary to manage moisture and acid gases.
Collect the gas sample in evacuated passivated silicon ceramic lined stainless-steel canisters, or equivalent.

Perform an MDL determination following the requirements in 40 CFR Part 136 Appendix B, including analyzing seven LBs filled with clean air and seven canisters filled with the VFC standard spiked within 2 to 10 times of the expected MDL.
Analyze each canister in a manner identical to the field samples.
Prepare a canister spiked with VFCs used for calibration (native target compounds) at the VFC specific MDLs.
Analyze this canister to confirm that target compounds meet the qualitative retention time identification criteria in Table OTM-50-4.
Prepare and analyze a canister spiked at the midpoint of the ICAL curve seven times.
Calculate the average percent recovery using the same set of replicate data generated for Section 9.2.3.5.
Establish a target concentration for the QRL based on the intended use of the method.
Assess the results from the lowest calibration concentration canister sample and determine if it meets accuracy criteria of ±30% of each target compound spike concentration.

Generate calibration gases by dynamic dilution of a certified compressed gas cylinder containing VFC targets in nitrogen to make a working standard that is diluted with a gas stream of humidified zero air in the heated mixing chamber.
Sample the gas standard mixture with a canister.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 7 in Section 12 of this method.
Connect a pressure transducer or gauge to a clean evacuated canister to monitor the canister pressure as gases are added.
Add the VFC calibration standard and the zero air diluent gases separately through a manifold or by direct connection of the gas to the standard canister or vessel.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 8 in Section 12 of this method.
Tune/optimize the MS to demonstrate acceptable performance across the selected ion mass range according to the manufacturer’s specifications.
Verify the MS tune according to the manufacturer’s specifications.
Perform optional BFB tune verification using criteria in Table OTM-50-4.
Add the IS through a dedicated non-sample port in the pre-concentrator at the same concentration for each injection.
Choose the quantitation ion for each IS compound as the most abundant ion (base peak).

Prepare two additional calibration standards at the lowest and highest points on the calibration curve generated in Section 10.1 and dilute the canisters with CO2 to final concentrations equal to or higher than the expected CO2 concentration in the field samples.
Use the stack equivalent concentration of CO2 determined using an ancillary measurement described in Section 8.6.3 to determine the percentage of CO2 to add to these calibration standards.
Analyze the two standards containing CO2 using the same injection volume that will be used to analyze samples.
Verify that recovery of each target compound in the two standards containing CO2 is within ±30% of the true value for the compound.
If either of the two CO2 spiked canister analyses generate VFC values exceeding ±30% for a given target compound, flag a quantitative value for that VFC with an explanation about CO2 interference.
Organize and load the canister samples for instrumental analysis.
Record sample names or identifiers, canister pressure, sample description, and total milliliters (mL) used for analysis in laboratory notebooks.
Connect samples to instrument sampler/autosampler and check for leaks.
Open the canister valves prior to starting the analytical sequence.
Re-tighten the connection and perform another leak check.
Generate an analytical sequence and sample batch that at a minimum includes one LB at the beginning, a daily CCV after the LB, and field samples (including one duplicate analysis).
Run the sample sequence.
At the conclusion of data acquisition, use the same software settings established during the calibration procedure to qualitatively identify analyte peaks in the predetermined retention time windows.
Confirm the identity of each analyte by comparing its retention time with that of the corresponding target analyte peak in an ICAL standard or CCV.
Confirm that the ions are consistent with the continuing calibration results.
Calculate canister volume sampled (Vc) using Equation 19: Vc = Pg/101.3 kPa x Cv.
Select ions based on ion abundance, uniqueness, and lack of interference with other target compounds and internal standards.
Perform a sample train leak check.
Verify zero flow from the outlet of the bypass pump.
If the leak check fails, inspect/tighten train components/connections and retry the leak check.
Perform a post-test sample train leak check.
Verify zero flow from the outlet of the bypass pump.
Void the test run if it does not pass the post-test leak check.

preconditions.text: If there is a regulatory or programmatic lowest quantitative reporting requirement, the laboratory calibration curve must be set at or below this level.
Allow the nominal standard mixture to flow and equilibrate with humidified zero air for a minimum of 30 minutes in the mixing chamber.
Upon initial installation of the instrument or following significant preventive maintenance or repair activities that impact the performance of the GC/MS system.
There is no spectral interference from a coeluting or nearby compound or interference.
The analyst must demonstrate that the response of the target analytes is not affected by the amount of CO2 in the canister samples during IDC and prior to each sample analysis.

If variable injection volumes are used to generate the calibration curve, the CO2 standards must be prepared in a way to yield the expected concentration at the injection volume used for sample canisters.

constraints.text: Source sampling teams should be trained and have experience with EPA Method 1, Method 2, Method 3, and Method 4.
Laboratory analysis teams should be trained and experienced in the use of gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC/MS) as described in EPA compendium Method TO-15A and ASTM D5466-21.
If modifying sample preparation portions, users must use GC/MS as the basis for separation and quantitation of method target compounds and meet all performance criteria in Section 9.
Field sampling and recovery staff must be trained in the best practices for extracting representative gas samples, conditioning the gas, and collecting the gas sample.
Connection tubing for the dynamic dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316) or silicon-ceramic coated stainless-steel.
Mixing chambers or manifolds must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic coated stainless-steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
Connection tubing for the static dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316).
The MDL study establishes the lowest detectable concentrations that are statistically different from the LB at 99% confidence.
Analysis results from these seven replicates must have a percent relative standard deviation (%RSD) of the concentrations no greater than 25% for all method analytes (Table OTM-50-4).
The average percent recovery for each analyte must be within ±30% of the concentration prepared in Section 9.2.3.5.
Lowest calibration concentrations must be no lower than three times the MDL determined in 9.2.3.3.
The final pressure of the calibration standard canister must not exceed the maximum pressure permitted by the GC/MS pre-concentrator unit.
The pressure transducer or gauge must meet the requirements in Section 9.2.1.6.

The concentration of IS added to each injection should be in the lower middle portion of the calibration range (e.g., 5 ppbv) but must not exceed the concentration of the highest calibration level (e.g., 20 ppbv).
CO2 may interfere with one or more target compounds.

Pre-concentrator settings, purge times and/or GC/MS operating conditions may be optimized to decrease the impact of CO2 interference at high concentration levels to avoid invalidating results for coeluting target compounds.

Carbon tetrafluoride (CF4) and trifluoromethane analyses may need to be performed using a multi-media cold trap or a separate (smaller) aliquot of gas from the sample canister to optimize the preconcentration procedure for the extreme volatility of CF4.
Field samples must be analyzed using the same injection volume used for calibration standards.
Do not open the canister valve if the leak check fails.
The signals for all characteristic masses shall be present and reach a maximum within the same two consecutive scans.
The retention time difference between the target compound in the sample and the CCV shall agree within ±6 scans or ±6 seconds (whichever is greater) of this difference in the CCV standard.
